title: Joint custody arrangements - are Indian courts finally adopting them
p1: Many believe divorce means one parent loses day-to-day connection with the child. But Indian courts are slowly questioning this old assumption. A child’s relationship with both parents is becoming a bigger part of custody decisions.
p2: The surprising part: joint custody does not always mean equal time with both parents. It usually means both parents share responsibilities and important decisions about the child’s education, health, and upbringing. Courts are increasingly looking beyond parental rights and focusing on the child’s best interests.
p3: Indian law does not have a separate joint custody framework like some countries, but courts have started exploring shared parenting models in suitable cases. The key factor is not what parents want, but what arrangement provides stability, care, and emotional support for the child. The future of custody may move from winning against each other to cooperating for the child.
conclusion: Should Indian courts move faster towards formal joint custody guidelines, or should each case continue to be decided individually?
